question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take (buyer authorization)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER call take() with taker=victim under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true and offer.buy=true making victim the buyer, via the call sequence attacker calls take(offer, ratifierData, units, victim, receiver, callback, callbackData) where payer is derived as msg.sender (attacker) when no callback, violating the invariant that credit increases must only occur with the account owner's consent, causing scoped impact: attacker forces victim into a credit position with associated pendingFee obligations without victim's knowledge? Proof idea: unit test where authorized operator takes a buy offer on behalf of victim and asserts victim's credit and pendingFee increase.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a lender call withdraw(market, units, lender, attacker) to drain the lender's credit to the attacker's address under the precondition that isAuthorized[lender][attacker]==true and lender has withdrawable credit, via the call sequence attacker calls withdraw(market, units, lender, attacker), violating the invariant that credit withdrawals must only benefit the account owner or explicitly designated receiver, causing scoped impact: attacker steals lender's loan tokens by redirecting withdrawal to attacker's address? Proof idea: unit test where authorized operator withdraws lender's credit to attacker's address and asserts attacker receives loan tokens.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + supplyCollateral] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a victim call supplyCollateral(market, index, assets, victim) to supply collateral on behalf of victim under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true and attacker has collateral tokens, via the call sequence attacker calls supplyCollateral(market, index, assets, victim) activating a new collateral index in victim's bitmap, violating the invariant that collateral activation must not be forced on a user without their consent, causing scoped impact: attacker activates a collateral with a reverting oracle for victim, making victim's account permanently unliquidatable and blocking withdrawCollateral? Proof idea: unit test where authorized operator activates a collateral with a reverting oracle and asserts victim's liquidate and withdrawCollateral revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + liquidate (liquidatorGate bypass)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a gated liquidator call liquidate() on behalf of the gated liquidator under the precondition that market.liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(gatedLiquidator)==true but canLiquidate(attacker)==false, via the call sequence attacker calls liquidate() directly (not on behalf of gatedLiquidator), violating the invariant that liquidatorGate.canLiquidate must be checked against msg.sender, causing scoped impact: attacker bypasses liquidator gate by impersonating an authorized liquidator? Proof idea: unit test with a liquidator gate that only allows a specific address, assert attacker cannot liquidate even if authorized for that address.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketSettlementFee (index=0 post-maturity)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is feeSetter set settlementFeeCbp0 to 0 for a market after maturity under the precondition that the market has passed maturity and post-maturity settlement fee is determined by index 0, via the call sequence feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, 0) after block.timestamp > market.maturity, then post-maturity takes have zero settlement fee, violating the invariant that post-maturity fees must remain non-zero to prevent fee-free settlement, causing scoped impact: feeSetter can eliminate all settlement fees post-maturity, reducing protocol revenue to zero for that market? Proof idea: unit test setting index-0 fee to 0 post-maturity and asserting settlementFee(id, 0) == 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized (address(0) as authorized)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER call setIsAuthorized(address(0), true, onBehalf) to set isAuthorized[onBehalf][address(0)]=true under the precondition that msg.sender==onBehalf or isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender]==true, via the call sequence setIsAuthorized(address(0), true, onBehalf), violating the invariant that address(0) must not be a valid authorized account since ecrecover returns address(0) on invalid signatures, causing scoped impact: if any code path checks isAuthorized[maker][address(0)], a failed ecrecover in EcrecoverRatifier could be treated as authorized? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified + isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER submit a signature that ecrecovers to an address that is authorized for the maker (but is not the maker) under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][signerAddress]==true and attacker can produce a valid EIP-712 signature from signerAddress, via the call sequence taker calls take() with ratifierData containing a signature from signerAddress, violating the invariant that offer authorization must be bound to the maker's explicit per-offer consent, causing scoped impact: any account authorized for the maker can sign and fill arbitrary offers on the maker's behalf without the maker signing each offer? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + flashLoan] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a victim use flashLoan to atomically borrow tokens and then call setIsAuthorized to escalate privileges under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true and the flashLoan callback can call setIsAuthorized, via the call sequence attacker calls flashLoan() with a callback that calls setIsAuthorized(newAttacker, true, victim), violating the invariant that flashLoan callbacks must not be able to mutate authorization state for third parties, causing scoped impact: attacker uses flashLoan callback to permanently grant new accounts authorization over victim's state? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketContinuousFee + claimContinuousFee (fee=0 then claim)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is feeSetter set continuousFee to 0 for a market and then feeClaimer attempts to claim continuousFeeCredit under the precondition that continuousFeeCredit > 0 from prior accrual and continuousFee is now 0, via the call sequence feeSetter calls setMarketContinuousFee(id, 0) then feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver), violating the invariant that setting fee to 0 must not affect already-accrued continuousFeeCredit, causing scoped impact: feeSetter can prevent future fee accrual while feeClaimer can still drain existing continuousFeeCredit, creating a race condition between fee setter and claimer? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take (offer.maker == taker check bypass)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for both maker and taker attempt to take an offer where offer.maker == taker under the precondition that isAuthorized[taker][attacker]==true and attacker calls take() with taker=taker and offer.maker=taker, via the call sequence attacker calls take(offer, ..., taker, ...) where offer.maker==taker, violating the invariant that self-takes must always revert regardless of authorization, causing scoped impact: attacker triggers a self-take on behalf of a user, potentially manipulating consumed state or credit/debt accounting? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + setIsAuthorized (circular authorization)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER establish a circular authorization chain A->B->A under the precondition that A authorizes B and B authorizes A, via the call sequence A calls setIsAuthorized(B, true, A) then B calls setIsAuthorized(A, true, B), violating the invariant that authorization chains must not create unexpected privilege amplification, causing scoped impact: A and B can each act on behalf of the other, effectively merging their Midnight state control and enabling cross-account manipulation? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setDefaultContinuousFee + touchMarket] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is feeSetter set defaultContinuousFee for a loan token to MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE immediately before a market is created under the precondition that a market for that loan token is about to be created, via the call sequence feeSetter calls setDefaultContinuousFee(loanToken, MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE) then touchMarket() is called creating the market with maximum continuous fee, violating the invariant that market creators should be able to predict the continuous fee their market will inherit, causing scoped impact: all lenders in the new market immediately accrue fees at the maximum rate, reducing their effective yield? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + liquidate (bad debt realization)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a borrower call liquidate() with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 to realize bad debt without seizing collateral under the precondition that isAuthorized[borrower][attacker]==true and borrower has bad debt (badDebt > 0), via the call sequence attacker calls liquidate(market, 0, 0, 0, borrower, false, receiver, address(0), ''), violating the invariant that bad debt realization must only be triggered by a liquidator (not an authorized operator acting on behalf of the borrower), causing scoped impact: attacker forces bad debt socialization on all lenders without the borrower's consent? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketSettlementFee + settlementFee interpolation (CBP rounding)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is feeSetter set adjacent breakpoints such that the piecewise linear interpolation in settlementFee() produces a value that is not a multiple of CBP under the precondition that feeLower and feeUpper are both multiples of CBP but their weighted average is not, via the call sequence feeSetter sets two adjacent breakpoints then a take() at a TTM between them computes a non-CBP-aligned fee, violating the invariant that settlement fees must be multiples of CBP to ensure consistent rounding, causing scoped impact: rounding in fee computation creates extractable value for takers who can time their trades to exploit fee interpolation discontinuities? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take (enterGate bypass via authorized operator)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a gated user call take() with taker=gatedUser to increase gatedUser's debt under the precondition that market.enterGate.canIncreaseDebt(gatedUser)==false but isAuthorized[gatedUser][attacker]==true, via the call sequence attacker calls take(offer, ..., gatedUser, ...) where offer.buy=false making gatedUser the seller, violating the invariant that enterGate must block debt increases for gated users regardless of who calls take(), causing scoped impact: attacker bypasses enterGate restriction by acting as an authorized operator for the gated user? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + multicall (authorization mutation between calls)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER use multicall to call setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, maker) in one call and then take() in a subsequent call within the same multicall batch under the precondition that attacker is authorized for maker, via the call sequence multicall([setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, maker), take(offerWithMaliciousRatifier, ...)]), violating the invariant that authorization state mutations and offer execution must be independently validated across multicall boundaries, causing scoped impact: attacker atomically grants and uses a malicious ratifier to fill arbitrary offers in a single transaction? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified (revocation race)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a maker call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to de-ratify an active root under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][attacker]==true and maker has active offers under root, via the call sequence attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false), violating the invariant that only the maker should be able to revoke ratification of their own offer roots, causing scoped impact: attacker front-runs a taker's take() to de-ratify the root, causing the take() to revert and DoS-ing the maker's liquidity? [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take (ratifier returning wrong value)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized for a maker set isAuthorized[maker][maliciousRatifier]=true where maliciousRatifier.isRatified() returns a value other than CALLBACK_SUCCESS under the precondition that the take() check is require(IRatifier(offer.ratifier).isRatified(...) == CALLBACK_SUCCESS), via the call sequence attacker calls setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true, maker) then take() with offer.ratifier=maliciousRatifier, violating the invariant that a ratifier returning a non-CALLBACK_SUCCESS value must always cause take() to revert, causing scoped impact: if maliciousRatifier returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS for any offer, all maker offers become fillable without per-offer validation?
